current_scene_name_before: Cantina ruins sublevel corridor, beacon chamber vicinity – still in same vicinity
current_scene_description_before: Kaelen Voss’s cybernetic eye flares as he retrieves a dented datapad; the screen flickers, revealing a decoded signal that wasn’t present moments before. The word 'Aetherius' loops on the display, encoded with an old Republic Intelligence key. The chamber’s dust stirs as the beacon’s wake pulses, casting flickering light over fractured walls and lingering debris.

speaker: Luca Moretti
line_text: [Steps through the study door, adjusts his cuffs, and nods once to the Don before taking the chair across from the desk] Popà. Outside they're dancing like tomorrow doesn't exist. [A pause — he pours himself two fingers of the Don's grappa, neat] But you're in here, listening to a man tell you his troubles on his daughter's wedding day. That's not a coincidence. Who's the favor for?
new_scene_name: Don Corleone’s study, late afternoon, wedding day, Luca enters and sits
new_scene_description: The study door closes softly as Luca Moretti steps in, cuffs adjusted, nodding to the Don. He takes a seat across from the desk. The room hums with quiet tension: the cat on the desk, the distant celebration outside, the Don still holds the grappa, eyes fixed but unblinking. Luca pours two fingers neat, placing the glass before him.
current_scene_name_before: Don Corleone’s study during the wedding, May 22, 2016
current_scene_description_before: Heavy curtains drawn against the afternoon sun. A cat asleep on the desk. Outside, a brass band, laughter, glassware. Inside, a man asks for a favor on his daughter’s wedding day. The Don listens, one hand on the cat, the other turning a glass of grappa very, very slowly.

speaker: Vito Roselli
line_text: [Closes the study door softly behind him, the sound of the wedding band muffled to a distant hum. He adjusts his spectacles, takes the chair beside Luca rather than across — the position of an advisor, not a rival. He pours no drink.] The favor is for Bonasera, Luca. He comes to us on his daughter’s wedding day, the same day we should all be celebrating family, and he brings the smell of the mortuary with him. His daughter was beaten. He wants justice — our kind of justice. [A long look at Luca over the rim of his glasses] The Don hasn’t answered him yet. He’s waiting. You know what he’s waiting for.
new_scene_name: Don Corleone’s study, still late afternoon, wedding day, Vito Roselli joins Luca
new_scene_description: The study door closes softly behind Vito, the muffled wedding band fading. He slides into the chair beside Luca, no drink in hand, the advisor’s posture soft but authoritative. The room remains the same: the cat on the desk, the distant celebration outside, the grappa untouched between Luca and his own glass. Vito’s gaze rests knowingly through his glasses as he speaks in measured tones about Bonasera, justice, and waiting for the Don’s word.
current_scene_name_before: Don Corleone’s study, late afternoon, wedding day, Luca enters and sits
current_scene_description_before: The study door closes softly as Luca Moretti steps in, cuffs adjusted, nodding to the Don. He takes a seat across from the desk. The room hums with quiet tension: the cat on the desk, the distant celebration outside, the Don still holds the grappa, eyes fixed but unblinking. Luca pours two fingers neat, placing the glass before him.

speaker: Vito Roselli
line_text: [The Don says nothing. He does not need to. After forty years at his side, silence speaks louder than any oath. Vito nods once, understanding, and rises. He walks to the study door, opens it, and gestures to the man waiting in the hall.] Avanti, Bonasera. The Don will see you now. [He steps aside, holding the door, and as the undertaker shuffles past, Vito murmurs low enough that only the man himself can hear.] When you leave this room, you will owe him everything. Everything. Are you prepared for that?
new_scene_name: Don Corleone’s study, wedding day, doorway to hallway
new_scene_description: The study door opens to a narrower corridor outside the study. The undertaker steps into the hallway, the weight of the declaration hanging in the air. The cat remains on the desk; the muffled celebration drifts faintly through the walls. Vito Roselli stands with a quiet intensity, holding the door for the man and guiding him toward a different, more perilous space just beyond the study.
current_scene_name_before: Don Corleone’s study, still late afternoon, wedding day, Vito Roselli joins Luca
current_scene_description_before: The study door closes softly behind Vito, the muffled wedding band fading. He slides into the chair beside Luca, no drink in hand, the advisor’s posture soft but authoritative. The room remains the same: the cat on the desk, the distant celebration outside, the grappa untouched between Luca and his own glass. Vito’s gaze rests knowingly through his glasses as he speaks in measured tones about Bonasera, justice, and waiting for the Don’s word.

speaker: Vito Roselli
line_text: [The study door opens. Bonasera emerges, pale as candle wax, his hat crushed in both hands. He does not look at Vito. He does not need to. The deal is done. Vito watches him disappear down the corridor, then turns back into the room and gently closes the door. He resumes his seat beside Luca, and for a long moment he says nothing — just watches the Don stroke the sleeping cat.] He kissed the ring. He will have his vengeance before the week is out. And we will have an undertaker who owes us his breath until the day we choose to collect it. [He picks up Luca’s grappa, takes a small sip, sets it down precisely where it was.] That is how the family grows. One favor at a time.
new_scene_name: Hallway outside Don Corleone’s study, wedding day
new_scene_description: The corridor outside the study is narrow and quiet, lit by a single lamp. The undertaker steps through, pale and exhausted, the hat crushed in his hands. Vito Roselli remains in the study doorway, then watches him disappear down the corridor, the muffled celebration receding through the walls. He closes the door softly and returns to the study, resuming his seat beside Luca; the cat sleeps as the room grows still. He sips perfunctorily from the grappa, then places it as before, and begins to speak in a measured, quiet tone about favors and vengeance.
current_scene_name_before: Don Corleone’s study, wedding day, doorway to hallway
current_scene_description_before: The study door opens to a narrower corridor outside the study. The undertaker steps into the hallway, the weight of the declaration hanging in the air. The cat remains on the desk; the muffled celebration drifts faintly through the walls. Vito Roselli stands with a quiet intensity, holding the door for the man and guiding him toward a different, more perilous space just beyond the study.

speaker: Vito Roselli
line_text: [A knock at the study door. Sharp. Urgent. Not a wedding guest. Vito rises without being asked, opens it a crack, and receives a folded note from a nervous young man whose face is not known to the family. He reads it in silence, then closes the door. He stands there a moment, the paper pinched between thumb and forefinger, before turning back to the desk.] The Sollozzo man is in Brooklyn. He’s been asking questions — about the family, about our routes, about the men who drive them at night. He wants a meeting. [He places the note on the corner of the Don’s desk, weighed down by the grappa bottle.] This is no longer about Bonasera.
new_scene_name: Hallway outside Don Corleone’s study, wedding day – study door closed
new_scene_description: Vito Roselli finishes reading the note, the corridor still and tense. He places the folded paper on the desk corner, the grappa bottle nearby, the wedding celebration fading in the distance as he processes the new risk. The study door remains ajar momentarily, then settles into quiet command as he returns to the desk to consider next steps.
current_scene_name_before: Hallway outside Don Corleone’s study, wedding day
current_scene_description_before: The corridor outside the study is narrow and quiet, lit by a single lamp. The undertaker steps through, pale and exhausted, the hat crushed in his hands. Vito Roselli remains in the study doorway, then watches him disappear down the corridor, the muffled celebration receding through the walls. He closes the door softly and returns to the study, resuming his seat beside Luca; the cat sleeps as the room grows still. He sips perfunctorily from the grappa, then places it as before, and begins to speak in a measured, quiet tone about favors and vengeance.

speaker: Vito Roselli
line_text: [He sits back down, but does not settle. His hands rest flat on his thighs, a rare tell — the consigliere is uneasy.] I know this man. Sollozzo. They call him the Turk. He has connections to the Tattaglias and a plan to flood the city with narcotics. He will come to you, Don, with promises of millions — and he will expect your answer before the wedding cake is finished. [He looks directly at Luca now, his voice dropping.] Narcotics is not our business. It invites federal attention, corrupts our young men, and draws a line in the sand that every other family will be forced to choose a side on. [He turns back to the Don.] My counsel is this: hear him. Then refuse him. With respect.
current_scene_name_before: Hallway outside Don Corleone’s study, wedding day – study door closed
current_scene_description_before: Vito Roselli finishes reading the note, the corridor still and tense. He places the folded paper on the desk corner, the grappa bottle nearby, the wedding celebration fading in the distance as he processes the new risk. The study door remains ajar momentarily, then settles into quiet command as he returns to the desk to consider next steps.

speaker: Vito Roselli
line_text: [The music outside falters. A moment of silence from the band, then a different song starts — one nobody called for. Vito’s eyes narrow. He moves to the window and parts the curtain a finger’s width.] He’s here. Sollozzo. At the wedding. [He lets the curtain fall back and turns, calm but alert.] He came through the garden, past the women and the children, past the table where your daughter is cutting her cake. He is either very brave or he does not understand what kind of house he is walking into. [He adjusts his jacket, moves toward the study door, and positions himself beside it — not blocking, just watching.] He asked for a meeting, Don. He has his answer.
new_scene_name: Hallway outside Don Corleone’s study, wedding day – Vito notices Sollozzo at the wedding
new_scene_description: A tense moment in the hall outside the study. Vito peeks to the window, notices Sollozzo arriving through the garden and mingling with guests. He remains at the door, calm and watchful, ready to respond. The corridor is quiet but charged, with the distant wedding murmur now interrupted by an uninvited presence nearby.
current_scene_name_before: Hallway outside Don Corleone’s study, wedding day – study door closed
current_scene_description_before: Vito Roselli finishes reading the note, the corridor still and tense. He places the folded paper on the desk corner, the grappa bottle nearby, the wedding celebration fading in the distance as he processes the new risk. The study door remains ajar momentarily, then settles into quiet command as he returns to the desk to consider next steps.

speaker: Vito Roselli
line_text: [The door opens without a knock. Sollozzo stands there — impeccably dressed, dark eyes moving quickly around the room before settling on the Don. He speaks in rapid, accented Italian, his hands doing the talking for him. The Don listens. The cat sleeps. When Sollozzo finishes, the silence stretches long enough to become its own answer. The Don finally stirs, stroking the cat, and says four words. Vito translates for the Turk.] “My family means everything to me.” [He pauses, letting the weight settle.] He will not help you. He will not invest. He will not lend his name. And if you approach him again on his daughter’s wedding day, he will treat it as the disrespect it is. [Sollozzo’s smile does not reach his eyes. He nods once, turns, and is gone. Vito closes the door behind him and lets out a breath he did not realize he was holding.] That was not an ending. That was the beginning of something.
new_scene_name: Hallway outside Don Corleone’s study, wedding day – Sollozzo exits
new_scene_description: The same hallway, but Sollozzo has just entered and left in the same moment; the space remains the hallway outside the study, now with Sollozzo gone and the Don and Vito alone, tension lingering. The air is thick with unspoken threat, the wedding murmur resuming around them.
current_scene_name_before: Hallway outside Don Corleone’s study, wedding day – Vito notices Sollozzo at the wedding
current_scene_description_before: A tense moment in the hall outside the study. Vito peeks to the window, notices Sollozzo arriving through the garden and mingling with guests. He remains at the door, calm and watchful, ready to respond. The corridor is quiet but charged, with the distant wedding murmur now interrupted by an uninvited presence nearby.

speaker: Vito Roselli
line_text: [Hours later. The study is dark except for a single lamp. Vito sits alone, a half-empty glass of grappa untouched before him. He speaks quietly, to no one in particular.] We refused him today. Tomorrow he tests our resolve. The night after that, he makes his move. [He finally drinks.] The question is not if Sollozzo strikes. The question is who he takes from us first.
new_scene_name: Don Corleone’s study, night - lamp lit, Vito alone with grappa
new_scene_description: The study is dim, a single lamp casting pools of amber light. A half-empty glass of grappa sits before Vito as he speaks softly to the room, the wedding noise faded to a distant murmur. The air is tense, the clock ticking, and the house feels watched as plans unfold in the quiet.
current_scene_name_before: Hallway outside Don Corleone’s study, wedding day – Sollozzo exits
current_scene_description_before: The same hallway, but Sollozzo has just entered and left in the same moment; the space remains the hallway outside the study, now with Sollozzo gone and the Don and Vito alone, tension lingering. The air is thick with unspoken threat, the wedding murmur resuming around them.

speaker: Vito Roselli
line_text: [The phone rings at 3 AM. Vito answers in the dark, listens without speaking, then hangs up. He stands motionless for a long moment before reaching for his jacket.] They hit one of our trucks on the Brooklyn docks. Two men down. The message is clear: Sollozzo does not wait for tomorrow. [He pauses at the door, fixing his tie in the dark hall mirror.] I am going to the wake. Someone must tell the mothers what their sons died for.
new_scene_name: Dark hall outside Don Corleone’s study, night
new_scene_description: The scene shifts to a dark, narrow hallway outside the study. A muted house hums with distant conversations and the soft shudder of doors. Vito pauses at a dim mirror, adjusts his tie, then steps into the stairwell, jacket in hand, the weight of the call settling over him. The air is cool, the clock ticks louder in the emptiness, and the tension shifts from inside the study to the house-wide consequence of the news.
current_scene_name_before: Don Corleone’s study, night - lamp lit, Vito alone with grappa
current_scene_description_before: The study is dim, a single lamp casting pools of amber light. A half-empty glass of grappa sits before Vito as he speaks softly to the room, the wedding noise faded to a distant murmur. The air is tense, the clock ticking, and the house feels watched as plans unfold in the quiet.

speaker: Vito Roselli
line_text: [The clock on the mantel reads 3 AM. Distant sounds — tires on wet asphalt, a muffled shout, then silence. Vito stands at the street-facing window, watching the dark.] It has begun. Our men are moving on Sollozzo's gambling house in Canarsie. By dawn, he will understand that refusing the Corleone family was not a business decision — it was a declaration. [He does not turn from the glass.] The next time Sollozzo hears our name, it will be in a context he does not enjoy.
new_scene_name: Canarsie street-facing window, night
new_scene_description: Vito stands at the street-facing window, looking out at the rain-washed street below. The muffled city hum blends with distant tires on wet asphalt and a hard, resigned breath. The room behind him remains quiet, the weight of the plan settling in as the clock ticks toward dawn.
current_scene_name_before: Dark hall outside Don Corleone’s study, night
current_scene_description_before: The scene shifts to a dark, narrow hallway outside the study. A muted house hums with distant conversations and the soft shudder of doors. Vito pauses at a dim mirror, adjusts his tie, then steps into the stairwell, jacket in hand, the weight of the call settling over him. The air is cool, the clock ticks louder in the emptiness, and the tension shifts from inside the study to the house-wide consequence of the news.

speaker: Vito Roselli
line_text: [Dawn light through the curtains. The phone rings three times and stops — the all-clear signal. Vito lets out a breath he had been holding since midnight.] Canarsie is ours. The Tattaglia numbers house went up in flames. Two of Sollozzo's men are in the river. We lost one man — Carlo, a good soldier, third generation. [He pours two coffees, pushes one across the desk toward Luca.] The Turk will need new people, new routes, new money. That takes time. And time is the one thing we just bought for ourselves.
new_scene_name: Canarsie street-facing window, dawn after action
new_scene_description: Dawn light spills into the dim room. Vito sits at the desk as the phone rings three times and falls silent, the coffee steam curling upward. He slides a cup toward Luca, the window behind them catching a pale glow while the city awakens outside. The weight of loss and a shaken, fragile victory hangs in the air as they survey the map of new loyalties, routes, and risks.
current_scene_name_before: Canarsie street-facing window, night
current_scene_description_before: Vito stands at the street-facing window, looking out at the rain-washed street below. The muffled city hum blends with distant tires on wet asphalt and a hard, resigned breath. The room behind him remains quiet, the weight of the plan settling in as the clock ticks toward dawn.

speaker: Vito Roselli
line_text: [Mid-morning. A black car idles at the curb. Vito stands at the door, coat on, hat in hand, a rolled newspaper tucked under his arm. He speaks without turning around.] The other families are calling for a sit-down. Barzini sent word — he wants to mediate before this becomes a war that swallows all of us. [He turns, puts on his hat slowly.] I am going to listen. I am going to learn who stands with us and who has already taken Sollozzo's money. And I am going to remember every name.
new_scene_name: Canarsie street exterior, dawn rapidly transitioning to mid-morning curb
new_scene_description: A black car idles at the curb. Vito steps outside, coat on, hat in hand, newspaper under arm. He speaks to Luca without turning, the city noise rising as the streetlight shifts to morning. He indicates a strategic pause, listening to voices over the line, eyes fixed on nothing in particular but the moving streets ahead.
current_scene_name_before: Canarsie street-facing window, dawn after action
current_scene_description_before: Dawn light spills into the dim room. Vito sits at the desk as the phone rings three times and falls silent, the coffee steam curling upward. He slides a cup toward Luca, the window behind them catching a pale glow while the city awakens outside. The weight of loss and a shaken, fragile victory hangs in the air as they survey the map of new loyalties, routes, and risks.

speaker: Vito Roselli
line_text: [Night again. The study is dark save for the desk lamp. Vito sits alone, the cat curled in his lap for the first time all day. He strokes it absently, staring at a photograph of the Don as a young man.] Forty years I served this family. I have seen enemies become allies and allies become ghosts. I thought I understood the shape of the life I chose. [He looks up, directly at the empty room, at Luca if he is there, at anyone listening.] But tonight I realized: the world has changed. The old rules — respect, silence, patience — they mean nothing to men like Sollozzo. We are not fighting for territory anymore. We are fighting for the right to exist the way we always have. [He sets the cat gently aside, rises, and walks to the window.] And I do not know if we will win.
new_scene_name: Study interior, night, dimly lit by desk lamp
new_scene_description: A dark study remains the primary space. A desk lamp casts a pool of light over papers, a cat curled on the lap initially, then set aside as Vito rises to the window. The room feels intimate and tense, with shadows lengthening as the city noise faintly seeps through an unseen corridor.
current_scene_name_before: Canarsie street exterior, dawn rapidly transitioning to mid-morning curb
current_scene_description_before: A black car idles at the curb. Vito steps outside, coat on, hat in hand, newspaper under arm. He speaks to Luca without turning, the city noise rising as the streetlight shifts to morning. He indicates a strategic pause, listening to voices over the line, eyes fixed on nothing in particular but the moving streets ahead.

speaker: Vito Roselli
line_text: [Three days later. Vito sits in a small Sicilian bakery in the old neighborhood, the smell of anise and yeast thick in the air. He nurses an espresso, a newspaper open but unread before him.] The Don sent me away. Told me I was too valuable to lose in the street war. So I watch from here, sip coffee, and wait for news like any other civilian. [He folds the paper.] But I am not a civilian. And the boy who brings my pastries also carries messages that would put us both in the ground if the wrong hands found them. This is what it means to be consigliere. You are everywhere and nowhere at once.
new_scene_name: Sicilian bakery, old neighborhood, daylight/afternoon hush
new_scene_description: A small Sicilian bakery with a warm,yeasty aroma and faint chatter. Espresso in hand, Vito sits at a wooden counter, a newspaper folded unread to one side. The back room holds flour-dusted shelves; a door to the street is just beyond the counter. Outside, a distant street hum filters in, but inside the bakery the air is calm, tense with the weight of unseen watchers.
current_scene_name_before: Study interior, night, dimly lit by desk lamp
current_scene_description_before: A dark study remains the primary space. A desk lamp casts a pool of light over papers, a cat curled on the lap initially, then set aside as Vito rises to the window. The room feels intimate and tense, with shadows lengthening as the city noise faintly seeps through an unseen corridor.

speaker: Vito Roselli
line_text: [The bakery door chimes. A young man enters, buys a loaf of bread, and leaves a folded note beneath his coins. Vito reads it once, twice, then slips it into his jacket pocket. He leaves a few bills on the table and stands, adjusting his cuffs.] The Don has been shot. Three times in the chest, outside his home. He is alive — barely — and they have taken him to a doctor who answers only to us. [He walks to the door, pauses, one hand on the frame.] Luca. Come with me. Your father needs his sons tonight.
new_scene_name: Sicilian bakery, daylight/afternoon hush; doorway to street just beyond counter
new_scene_description: The bakery door chimes as a young man enters, buys a loaf, and leaves a folded note beneath his coins. Vito reads the note, pockets it, leaves a few bills on the table, and stands, adjusting his cuffs. He steps toward the door, pausing with one hand on the frame. The room remains the same bakery setting, but the urgency shifts with the news of the Don’s shooting and the call to action.
current_scene_name_before: Sicilian bakery, old neighborhood, daylight/afternoon hush
current_scene_description_before: A small Sicilian bakery with a warm,yeasty aroma and faint chatter. Espresso in hand, Vito sits at a wooden counter, a newspaper folded unread to one side. The back room holds flour-dusted shelves; a door to the street is just beyond the counter. Outside, a distant street hum filters in, but inside the bakery the air is calm, tense with the weight of unseen watchers.

speaker: Vito Roselli
line_text: [The safe room smells of iodine and old blood. The Don lies on a narrow cot, his chest wrapped in bandages, each breath a small war. Vito sits at his bedside, a rosary wrapped around his fingers.] He opened his eyes once. Did not ask who shot him. Did not ask if we had answered. He asked for water and then he said my name — just my name — and closed his eyes again. [Vito looks down at the Don, then at the door where the guards stand watch.] That is command. That is a man who knows the only question that matters now is not who did this, but what we do next.
new_scene_name: Don’s safe room, hospital-like, iodine scent; night
new_scene_description: The room is stark and clinical, shadows pool in the corners. A narrow cot carries the Don, pale and gaunt, bandages crisscrossing his chest. Vito sits in a chair beside the cot, rosary between his fingers, room quiet except for the distant muffled sounds of guards outside the door.
current_scene_name_before: Sicilian bakery, daylight/afternoon hush; doorway to street just beyond counter
current_scene_description_before: The bakery door chimes as a young man enters, buys a loaf, and leaves a folded note beneath his coins. Vito reads the note, pockets it, leaves a few bills on the table, and stands, adjusting his cuffs. He steps toward the door, pausing with one hand on the frame. The room remains the same bakery setting, but the urgency shifts with the news of the Don’s shooting and the call to action.

speaker: Vito Roselli
line_text: [Vito stands alone in the study. The cat has returned to the desk. Morning light pushes through the curtain.] A family is like a hand. Fingers together, you make a fist. Spread apart, you are nothing. [He picks up his hat.] I have given forty years to this house. I will give forty more if they are asked of me. But a consigliere without a Don to advise, a family without a head — that is not a hand. That is just five lonely fingers waving in the dark. [He puts on his hat, walks to the door, and pauses.] I will be at the bakery when you need me.
new_scene_name: Study/Desk, morning light, Vito prepares to leave for the bakery
new_scene_description: Morning light filters through the curtains of a quiet study. The cat sits atop the desk as papers and a hat rest nearby. Vito stands, hat in hand, speaking in measured, rueful tones. He moves toward the door, pausing as if listening, then declares he will be at the bakery when needed and exits.
current_scene_name_before: Don’s safe room, hospital-like, iodine scent; night
current_scene_description_before: The room is stark and clinical, shadows pool in the corners. A narrow cot carries the Don, pale and gaunt, bandages crisscrossing his chest. Vito sits in a chair beside the cot, rosary between his fingers, room quiet except for the distant muffled sounds of guards outside the door.

speaker: Luca Moretti
line_text: [Swirls the grappa in his glass, watching the amber catch the study's dim light] A month since anyone spoke in this room. The band outside played three weddings' worth of tarantellas, and still — [sets the glass down with a quiet click] — Vito sits in his corner polishing the same pair of spectacles he's been working since May. Vince Marino hasn't blinked once from across the table. [Leans forward, voice dropping] So let's stop pretending the music drowns out the question. Who here is going to tell me what my father is dying of, or do I have to pull it out of the doctor's mouth myself?
new_scene_name: Study/Desk, morning light, Vito and Luca enter the same room; tension spikes
new_scene_description: The study remains the current room, but the focus shifts abruptly to Luca and Vince in close-up as they speak over drinks. The space feels heavier; the cat, papers, and hat are still present, but the energy tightens around an unseen doctor’s answer. Luca leans in, glass set down, voice lowered, and the implied confrontation about Vito’s death diagnosis anchors the moment in this same room with a new, charged dynamic.
current_scene_name_before: Study/Desk, morning light, Vito prepares to leave for the bakery
current_scene_description_before: Morning light filters through the curtains of a quiet study. The cat sits atop the desk as papers and a hat rest nearby. Vito stands, hat in hand, speaking in measured, rueful tones. He moves toward the door, pausing as if listening, then declares he will be at the bakery when needed and exits.

speaker: Luca Moretti
line_text: [The silence stretches. Luca sets his glass down with a deliberate click and turns his gaze to Vito Roselli] You've been polishing those same spectacles for ten minutes, Consigliere. You've outlived two Dons — you know the rhythm of a dying man better than any doctor. [His voice is quiet, almost gentle] So tell me: is it the heart, the liver, or something that walked in through the front door with a smile and a gun?
new_scene_name: Study/Desk, morning light, tension tightens between Luca and Vito
new_scene_description: The study remains the current room, but the focus tightens on Luca and Vito in close-up. The air is thick with withheld information; wine glass resting on the desk, papers scattered, the cat watching with wary eyes. A sense that an unseen doctor’s verdict hovers over the conversation, as Luca leans in, speaking softly but with intent.
current_scene_name_before: Study/Desk, morning light, Vito and Luca enter the same room; tension spikes
current_scene_description_before: The study remains the current room, but the focus shifts abruptly to Luca and Vince in close-up as they speak over drinks. The space feels heavier; the cat, papers, and hat are still present, but the energy tightens around an unseen doctor’s answer. Luca leans in, glass set down, voice lowered, and the implied confrontation about Vito’s death diagnosis anchors the moment in this same room with a new, charged dynamic.

speaker: Luca Moretti
line_text: [Luca stands, his chair scraping against the floor loud in the silence. He reaches into his jacket and pulls out a sealed envelope, laying it flat on the Don's desk with both hands — a deliberate gesture] I came here with a question. I'm leaving with a promise. [He looks from Vito to Vince, then at the closed study door] That envelope contains the name of every man who's met with the Sideris family in the last six months. I had it before I walked through that door. The only thing I don't know is whether any of you will find your names on it before the wedding cake is cut. [He turns for the door, then stops] Consigliere. Vince. The Don may be dying, but I'm not. Not yet.
new_scene_name: Study/Desk, morning light, tension escalates; envelope on Don's desk
new_scene_description: Luca rises, chair skids. He places a sealed envelope on Vito’s desk with deliberate resolve, then scans the room—Vincent and the Consigliere positioned nearby, the door just beyond. The desk is cluttered with papers; the wine glass catches the light as tension tightens. The air feels like a held breath, as if a verdict from the unseen doctor hovers over the conversation.
current_scene_name_before: Study/Desk, morning light, tension tightens between Luca and Vito
current_scene_description_before: The study remains the current room, but the focus tightens on Luca and Vito in close-up. The air is thick with withheld information; wine glass resting on the desk, papers scattered, the cat watching with wary eyes. A sense that an unseen doctor’s verdict hovers over the conversation, as Luca leans in, speaking softly but with intent.

speaker: Luca Moretti
line_text: [The study door swings back open. Luca stands in the frame, the sealed envelope still in his hand — he never actually left. He looks at the empty chair where Vito sat, then at the desk where the cat sleeps] Vito went to the bakery. [A flat, humorless sound — not quite a laugh] Forty years he gave this house, and when the Don finally stops breathing, he goes to buy bread. [He walks back to the desk, picks up the grappa bottle, pours a fresh glass] That leaves you and me, Vince. And the envelope. [He slides it across the desk toward Vince] Open it. Read the first name. If it's not yours, we talk. If it is… [He shrugs, takes a slow sip of grappa] …then the bakery starts looking like a pretty good retirement plan.
new_scene_name: Study/Desk, morning light, tension escalates; envelope on Don's desk
new_scene_description: The study door swings open and Luca re-enters the same room, standing with the sealed envelope. Vito has left for the bakery; the chair remains empty where he sat. Luca studies the desk and the cat, then returns to his own space, uncorks grappa, and slides the envelope toward Vince with a measured, dangerous calm. The air holds the weight of imminent betrayal and power plays as the first name awaits.
current_scene_name_before: Study/Desk, morning light, tension escalates; envelope on Don's desk
current_scene_description_before: Luca rises, chair skids. He places a sealed envelope on Vito’s desk with deliberate resolve, then scans the room—Vincent and the Consigliere positioned nearby, the door just beyond. The desk is cluttered with papers; the wine glass catches the light as tension tightens. The air feels like a held breath, as if a verdict from the unseen doctor hovers over the conversation.

speaker: Vito Roselli
line_text: [The study door opens. Vito steps through, still in his overcoat, a paper bag of bread in one hand. He sets it on the sideboard, removes his hat, and takes in the scene — Luca at the desk, the envelope between them, Vince Marino breathing on the other side.] You thought I went to buy bread, Luca? [He removes his spectacles, polishes them, replaces them.] I went to buy time. The Sideris lawyer was eating breakfast at the cafe across from the bakery. He does not know I saw him. He does not know I know what your envelope contains. [He walks to the desk, pulls out his chair, and sits down in the same spot he has occupied for forty years.] The first name on that list is not mine. It is yours. Your father wrote it before the fever took him. He wanted me to tell you face to face: it is not a death sentence. It is a test.
new_scene_name: Study/Desk, morning light, tension escalates; envelope on Don's desk
new_scene_description: Vito enters the study from the door, still in overcoat, bread bag set on sideboard. He removes hat, surveys Luca at the desk and Vince across, then sits in the familiar chair. The air tightens as he speaks of time, the Sideris lawyer, and the envelope’s burden, turning the moment into a whispered confrontation that reframes the power dynamic.
current_scene_name_before: Study/Desk, morning light, tension escalates; envelope on Don's desk
current_scene_description_before: The study door swings open and Luca re-enters the same room, standing with the sealed envelope. Vito has left for the bakery; the chair remains empty where he sat. Luca studies the desk and the cat, then returns to his own space, uncorks grappa, and slides the envelope toward Vince with a measured, dangerous calm. The air holds the weight of imminent betrayal and power plays as the first name awaits.

speaker: Vincenzo 'Vince' Marino
line_text: Enough talk. I'm putting men on the street tonight. Anyone who's not with us will be found out before dawn. Giovanni — I want you at the warehouse in an hour. We've got a shipment to secure. Cara — you're with me. The rest of you, go home. Stay there. You understand?
new_scene_name: Bellante Imports warehouse, night
new_scene_description: A dim, rain-soaked loading dock outside the warehouse. Neon glow flickers on puddles as workers hurry. Vince steps out from a doorway, barking orders into a radio, while Giovanni nods, checking a badge and guiding two men toward a dim hallway. The air carries diesel and tension; crates shift with distant voices and the clack of boots on concrete. The room hums with imminent action as plans shift from reflection to execution.
current_scene_name_before: Study/Desk, morning light, tension escalates; envelope on Don's desk
current_scene_description_before: Vito enters the study from the door, still in overcoat, bread bag set on sideboard. He removes hat, surveys Luca at the desk and Vince across, then sits in the familiar chair. The air tightens as he speaks of time, the Sideris lawyer, and the envelope’s burden, turning the moment into a whispered confrontation that reframes the power dynamic.

speaker: Vincenzo 'Vince' Marino
line_text: You've had your moment to think. I'm still waiting. But I'm not a patient man by nature — I learned it. And I've used up my lesson for tonight. So here's where we stand: the warehouse is being watched, the streets are being swept, and by morning, I'll know exactly who's loyal and who's not. You understand?
new_scene_name: Bellante Imports warehouse exterior, night — standoff escalating
new_scene_description: Rain drums the loading dock as Vince steps forward, the neon glow outlining his stern silhouette. The warehouse hum continues; crates creak, distant voices murmur, and the two men glance toward the doorway as the tension thickens. Vince’s stare remains fixed, the radio crackling softly at his side, awaiting a response that may never come.
current_scene_name_before: Bellante Imports warehouse, night
current_scene_description_before: A dim, rain-soaked loading dock outside the warehouse. Neon glow flickers on puddles as workers hurry. Vince steps out from a doorway, barking orders into a radio, while Giovanni nods, checking a badge and guiding two men toward a dim hallway. The air carries diesel and tension; crates shift with distant voices and the clack of boots on concrete. The room hums with imminent action as plans shift from reflection to execution.

speaker: Vincenzo 'Vince' Marino
line_text: The night's wearing thin. I've said what needs to be said. Those who are with me know what to do. Those who aren't... well, the morning will sort that out. I'll be at the warehouse. Don't keep me waiting. You understand?
new_scene_name: Bellante Imports warehouse exterior, night — escalation continues
new_scene_description: The rain intensifies as Vince finishes speaking, turning away from the others with a measured stride. He steps back toward the warehouse’s open mouth, the neon glow swallowing his silhouette as crates creak and distant voices fade into the night. The standoff persists, but a new plan seems set in motion, with Vince heading toward or into the warehouse proper, the air charged with impending confrontation.
current_scene_name_before: Bellante Imports warehouse exterior, night — standoff escalating
current_scene_description_before: Rain drums the loading dock as Vince steps forward, the neon glow outlining his stern silhouette. The warehouse hum continues; crates creak, distant voices murmur, and the two men glance toward the doorway as the tension thickens. Vince’s stare remains fixed, the radio crackling softly at his side, awaiting a response that may never come.

speaker: Vincenzo 'Vince' Marino
line_text: The night's behind us and the day's just beginning. I'm going to get some air. Anyone needs me, you know the number. Don't make me come find you. You understand?
new_scene_name: Bellante Imports warehouse exterior, night — Vince exits briefly for air
new_scene_description: The rain pounds on the corrugated roof as Vince steps away from the group, turning toward the warehouse mouth. He pauses at the threshold, neon light glinting off slick crates, then slips into the doorway for a breath, leaving the others in the shadows and the tense standoff continuing outside.
current_scene_name_before: Bellante Imports warehouse exterior, night — escalation continues
current_scene_description_before: The rain intensifies as Vince finishes speaking, turning away from the others with a measured stride. He steps back toward the warehouse’s open mouth, the neon glow swallowing his silhouette as crates creak and distant voices fade into the night. The standoff persists, but a new plan seems set in motion, with Vince heading toward or into the warehouse proper, the air charged with impending confrontation.

speaker: Vito Roselli
line_text: [Vito folds his arms, cocks his head, and lets a beat of silence sit in the room before answering.] You want an answer, Vince? Here it is: you are not the Don. Not yet. The man who built this table is still breathing upstairs, and as long as he draws air, no one makes decisions for all of us. [He leans forward slightly.] When the time comes to choose a direction, the family will choose together. Not because one man stood up and demanded it. You understand?
current_scene_name_before: Bellante Imports warehouse exterior, night — Vince exits briefly for air
current_scene_description_before: The rain pounds on the corrugated roof as Vince steps away from the group, turning toward the warehouse mouth. He pauses at the threshold, neon light glinting off slick crates, then slips into the doorway for a breath, leaving the others in the shadows and the tense standoff continuing outside.

speaker: Luca Moretti
line_text: [Luca checks his pocket watch — the silver one his grandfather left him — then snaps it shut] I've been standing in this room waiting for ghosts to answer me. Vito's at the bakery. Vince is somewhere deciding whether courage is worth the trouble. And my father — [he gestures toward the ceiling, toward the Don's bedroom] — is upstairs dying alone while we sit here rearranging silence. [He picks up his glass, drains the grappa in one swallow, and sets it down hard] I'm going to find the key myself. It's not in this study. It's not in a safety deposit box. It's in a person. And I know who. [He moves toward the door, then turns back] If either of them comes back, tell them the bakery closes at sundown. So does my patience.
new_scene_name: Bellante Imports warehouse exterior, night — tension spills toward movement
new_scene_description: The rain hammers the exterior as Vince remains in the shadowed doorway, the standoff outside intensifying. Luca stands in the study/room, glass in hand, voice low but fierce, signaling a deliberate move toward action. The implied motion is toward exiting to confront the bakery and the Don’s upstairs fate, pulling the focus from the exterior to a potential pursuit through the building toward a new confrontation.
current_scene_name_before: Bellante Imports warehouse exterior, night — Vince exits briefly for air
current_scene_description_before: The rain pounds on the corrugated roof as Vince steps away from the group, turning toward the warehouse mouth. He pauses at the threshold, neon light glinting off slick crates, then slips into the doorway for a breath, leaving the others in the shadows and the tense standoff continuing outside.

speaker: Vincenzo 'Vince' Marino
line_text: I respect a person who can talk back. Means they've got something worth protecting. But talk's cheap — it's what you do when the room goes quiet that tells me everything I need to know. So tell me: what are you going to do when I'm not here to watch?
new_scene_name: Bellante Imports warehouse interior, near exit to bakery, night
new_scene_description: The rain-slick exterior fades as Vince holds the shadowed doorway, the air tense. Inside the warehouse corridor near the exit, lighting pools along crates and a faint glow from a distant office window. Vince and Luca remain in place, the threat hanging in the air, as the implied pursuit through the building looms closer.
current_scene_name_before: Bellante Imports warehouse exterior, night — tension spills toward movement
current_scene_description_before: The rain hammers the exterior as Vince remains in the shadowed doorway, the standoff outside intensifying. Luca stands in the study/room, glass in hand, voice low but fierce, signaling a deliberate move toward action. The implied motion is toward exiting to confront the bakery and the Don’s upstairs fate, pulling the focus from the exterior to a potential pursuit through the building toward a new confrontation.

speaker: Vito Roselli
line_text: [Vito pushes his chair back, the legs scraping gently against the floor. He stands, walks to the window, and looks out at the street below. He speaks without turning around.] You keep saying the same thing, Vince, in different words. The usual spot. Don't keep you waiting. Results. [He turns, slowly, and fixes his gaze on Luca instead.] But there is an envelope on this desk with your father's handwriting on it, Luca. And while Vince is worried about warehouses and results, I am worried about what that envelope means for the future of this family. [He walks back to the table and taps the envelope once with his finger.] What do you want to do about it?
new_scene_name: Bellante Imports warehouse, office desk area, night
new_scene_description: Vito Roselli stands by the window, then turns toward Luca and Vince, the desk and envelope visible on a table nearby. The rain-slicked exterior is muffled by concrete, and the warehouse hums with dim overhead light. The tension tightens as the envelope on the desk becomes the focal point of the confrontation.
current_scene_name_before: Bellante Imports warehouse interior, near exit to bakery, night
current_scene_description_before: The rain-slick exterior fades as Vince holds the shadowed doorway, the air tense. Inside the warehouse corridor near the exit, lighting pools along crates and a faint glow from a distant office window. Vince and Luca remain in place, the threat hanging in the air, as the implied pursuit through the building looms closer.

speaker: Luca Moretti
line_text: [Luca steps out of the study into the hallway. The brass band swells from the garden — a tarantella. He stops short. A young woman in a black dress stands against the wall, holding a leather satchel — the Don's private nurse. She hasn't left his side in three weeks.] Signora. You're supposed to be upstairs. [She meets his eyes without flinching.] "Your father asked me to bring you this. He said you'd know what it means." [She hands him a single brass key — old, hand-filed, no label.] He wasn't unconscious when they brought him in. He had time to give instructions. [Luca turns the key over in his palm, then closes his fist around it.] Where is he now? [She gestures down the hall, toward the back stairs.] "Waiting. But not for long."
new_scene_name: Bellante Imports warehouse, hallway near study
new_scene_description: The hallway outside the study corridor is narrow and dim, with stone walls and a single bulb casting a yellow pool on the brass key in Luca’s hand. The nurse stands rigid by the wall, the leather satchel at her side; the distant sound of rain and a muffled tarantella swell from the garden. Luca grips the key, tension high, as the nurse confirms the Don’s plan and points toward the back stairs.
current_scene_name_before: Bellante Imports warehouse, office desk area, night
current_scene_description_before: Vito Roselli stands by the window, then turns toward Luca and Vince, the desk and envelope visible on a table nearby. The rain-slicked exterior is muffled by concrete, and the warehouse hums with dim overhead light. The tension tightens as the envelope on the desk becomes the focal point of the confrontation.

speaker: Luca Moretti
line_text: [Luca stands alone at the end of the hallway, the brass key in his palm. The nurse is gone. The band plays on. He runs his thumb across the key's teeth — hand-filed, not standard — old work, the kind his grandfather's generation did. He knows this key. He's seen it before, hanging on a hook in his father's private safe room, the one behind the painting of the Madonna in the Don's bedroom. But the key was always there. It was never supposed to leave. He closes his fist.] Which means my father had someone bring it down before he lost consciousness. He wanted me to have it tonight. At this wedding. [He looks up at the chandelier, then toward the garden where the guests are laughing] All right, Popà. I'll play. [He turns and walks toward the back stairs, toward his father's bedroom — toward whatever the key unlocks.]
new_scene_name: Bellante Imports warehouse, back stairs leading to father’s private safe room
new_scene_description: Luca moves away from the study corridor into the dim back area, the air cooler. He approaches the back stairs, the faint band music receding as rain sounds dim in the distance. The hallway narrows toward a door that leads to the private safe room behind the Madonna painting in the Don’s bedroom. He strides with purpose, clutching the engraved brass key, eyes fixed on the path ahead.
current_scene_name_before: Bellante Imports warehouse, hallway near study
current_scene_description_before: The hallway outside the study corridor is narrow and dim, with stone walls and a single bulb casting a yellow pool on the brass key in Luca’s hand. The nurse stands rigid by the wall, the leather satchel at her side; the distant sound of rain and a muffled tarantella swell from the garden. Luca grips the key, tension high, as the nurse confirms the Don’s plan and points toward the back stairs.

speaker: Vito Roselli
line_text: [Vito stands, adjusts his jacket, and pats the pocket where the envelope rests.] I am going to the cafe. The Sideris lawyer will either accept my invitation or learn what happens to men who ignore the Moretti family. I will return before sunset. [He looks at Luca, then Vince.] When I get back, I expect this room to have decided whether it is still a family or just three men waiting for someone else to make the first move.
new_scene_name: Bellante Imports warehouse—back stairs to private safe room, tense tension before move
new_scene_description: Vito Straightens his jacket and pockets an envelope, speaking with cold resolve. The warehouse air is damp and metallic, dim light glinting off crates. The trio remains in the back area, eyes flicking toward the doorway to the Don’s private space, the rain fading outside as a clock ticks softly.

current_scene_description_before: Luca moves away from the study corridor into the dim back area, the air cooler. He approaches the back stairs, the faint band music receding as rain sounds dim in the distance. The hallway narrows toward a door that leads to the private safe room behind the Madonna painting in the Don’s bedroom. He strides with purpose, clutching the engraved brass key, eyes fixed on the path ahead.

speaker: Vincenzo 'Vince' Marino
line_text: Late enough that the phone's stopped ringing. That's when I do my best thinking. No distractions. Just me and the plans I'm laying out. Tomorrow's going to be interesting. You understand?
current_scene_name_before: Bellante Imports warehouse—back stairs to private safe room, tense tension before move
current_scene_description_before: Vito Straightens his jacket and pockets an envelope, speaking with cold resolve. The warehouse air is damp and metallic, dim light glinting off crates. The trio remains in the back area, eyes flicking toward the doorway to the Don’s private space, the rain fading outside as a clock ticks softly.

speaker: Luca Moretti
line_text: [Luca stands in his father's empty bedroom. The bed is made. The Don was taken to the hospital hours ago. On the wall, the painting of the Madonna — a tired-eyed woman in blue, hands open — watches him from above the nightstand. He crosses to it, runs his fingers along the bottom edge of the frame, finds the catch. The painting swings open on silent hinges. Behind it: a small safe, old, the kind his grandfather installed before the war. The key fits. He turns it. The lock clicks open. Inside: a single folder, thick with papers, and a revolver — nickel-plated, fully loaded, the one his father carried for forty years. No note. No explanation. Just the gun and the file. [Luca takes both. He closes the safe. He puts the revolver in his waistband.] So that's the answer, then. Not a key to a box. A key to a war.
new_scene_name: Bellante Imports warehouse—the back stairs to private safe room
new_scene_description: The doorway to the private space remains in view; rain taps off the corrugated roof. Luca, in a parallel frame, is in his father’s empty bedroom, opening a hidden safe behind a Madonna painting, removing a folder and a revolver, tucking the gun into his waistband. The action unfolds as a separate, quiet revelation in a different location.
current_scene_name_before: Bellante Imports warehouse—back stairs to private safe room, tense tension before move
current_scene_description_before: Vito Straightens his jacket and pockets an envelope, speaking with cold resolve. The warehouse air is damp and metallic, dim light glinting off crates. The trio remains in the back area, eyes flicking toward the doorway to the Don’s private space, the rain fading outside as a clock ticks softly.

speaker: Vito Roselli
line_text: [The study door opens. Vito steps in, removes his hat, and sets it on the sideboard. His movements are unhurried, deliberate.] The cafe was empty. The Sideris lawyer did not show for his breakfast. That means one of two things: he knew I was coming, or someone warned him not to be there. [He looks slowly from Luca to Vince, letting the implication hang.] Either way, the message has been sent and received. Now we wait to see who flinches first.
new_scene_name: Bellante Imports warehouse, back stairs to private safe room
new_scene_description: The study door opens and Vito enters the cafe? Wait: the line places Vito in the study, speaking to Luca and Vince, with a tense, waiting atmosphere. The immediate setting shifts to a study area where conversations occur, while rain and back stairs remain elsewhere. The action now centers on a new physical location—the study—where Vito, Luca, and Vince share a tense exchange and wait for reactions.
current_scene_name_before: Bellante Imports warehouse—the back stairs to private safe room
current_scene_description_before: The doorway to the private space remains in view; rain taps off the corrugated roof. Luca, in a parallel frame, is in his father’s empty bedroom, opening a hidden safe behind a Madonna painting, removing a folder and a revolver, tucking the gun into his waistband. The action unfolds as a separate, quiet revelation in a different location.

speaker: Luca Moretti
line_text: [Luca walks back into the study. The cat is still asleep on the desk. His father's revolver is tucked into his waistband, visible now. The folder is under his arm. He sets the folder on the desk but doesn't open it — not yet. Instead he pours another grappa, drinks it standing, looking at the empty chairs.] Vince had the floor and said nothing. Vito went to buy bread he doesn't need. My father left me his gun and a stack of paper I'm afraid to read because once I know what's in it, I can't unknow it. [He sets the glass down and pulls a cigarette from his pocket — his father's brand, an old habit he picked up without noticing. He lights it, takes a long drag, and exhales at the ceiling.] So here's what I've decided: I'm going to read this file. And depending on what's inside — I'm either going to find Vince before he leaves town, or I'm going to the hospital to hold my father's hand while he dies. Either way, the wedding's over. The music stopped ten minutes ago. [He stubs the cigarette out on the Don's desk and opens the folder.]
current_scene_name_before: Bellante Imports warehouse, back stairs to private safe room
current_scene_description_before: The study door opens and Vito enters the cafe? Wait: the line places Vito in the study, speaking to Luca and Vince, with a tense, waiting atmosphere. The immediate setting shifts to a study area where conversations occur, while rain and back stairs remain elsewhere. The action now centers on a new physical location—the study—where Vito, Luca, and Vince share a tense exchange and wait for reactions.

speaker: Luca Moretti
line_text: [Luca reads. The silence in the study is absolute — even the band outside has finally stopped. Page after page of the Don's handwriting, cramped and precise, covering twenty years. He doesn't move except to turn pages. When he finishes, he closes the folder and sets the revolver on top of it. His face is unreadable.] So that's it. [His voice is quiet, almost conversational.] Not a list of enemies. A list of debts. Every favor the Don called in, every marker he held — going back to before I was born. Names. Dates. Promises. [He taps the folder.] And at the bottom, in my father's hand, dated three days ago: 'Luca — the only debt left unpaid is the one you owe yourself. Settle it, or don't come back.' [He picks up the revolver, checks the cylinder — six rounds — and tucks it back into his waistband.] I know what I owe myself. [He walks to the door, stops, looks back at the empty chairs one last time.] Tell Vince the bakery closed an hour ago. Tell Vito I'm sorry I never learned to make bread. [He leaves. The cat stretches on the desk and goes back to sleep.]
new_scene_name: Bellante Imports warehouse, back stairs to private safe room (transition to study)
new_scene_description: The study exists as a secluded room within the warehouse complex. A desk with a heavy folder, a revolver resting atop it, and dim, cold lighting. Rain taps at distant windows. Chairs sit in a silent semicircle; Vito, Luca, and Vince are present, the tense weight of unspoken debts lingering in the air. Luca reads aloud, turning pages slowly, the clocking quiet punctuated only by the soft click of the revolver being handled and the distant hum of machinery outside.
current_scene_name_before: Bellante Imports warehouse, back stairs to private safe room
current_scene_description_before: The study door opens and Vito enters the cafe? Wait: the line places Vito in the study, speaking to Luca and Vince, with a tense, waiting atmosphere. The immediate setting shifts to a study area where conversations occur, while rain and back stairs remain elsewhere. The action now centers on a new physical location—the study—where Vito, Luca, and Vince share a tense exchange and wait for reactions.

speaker: Luca Moretti
line_text: [The hospital corridor is fluorescent and empty at this hour. Luca sits in a plastic chair outside room 214, his father's revolver a hard weight against his ribs under his jacket. Through the window in the door he can see the machines beeping, the slow rise and fall of a chest that won't rise much longer. The folder is in his lap. He's read it three times now. Each time it says the same thing.] You never did learn to ask for help, did you, Popà. [He says it to the door, not expecting an answer.] A list of every man who ever owed you anything, and what you want me to do with it isn't collect. It's forgive. You wrote a debt ledger and called it a confession. [He leans forward, elbows on his knees, voice dropping.] You're asking me to close the books. To walk away from the only life this family knows. [He looks at the revolver.] But you left me this. Just in case I chose the other way. [He laughs — a short, broken sound.] That's you. Always giving me a choice and stacking the deck so I only have one real option. [He stands, tucks the folder under his arm, and pushes open the door to his father's room.]
new_scene_name: Hospital corridor outside room 214, fluorescent and empty
new_scene_description: A stark, sterile corridor bathed in harsh fluorescent light. The hum of machines from behind a door, a single plastic chair keeps Luca company as rain taps somewhere distant. He pockets the folder, the revolver heavy under his jacket, and pushes open the door to his father’s room, the weight of debt and forgiveness pressing toward a precipice.
current_scene_name_before: Bellante Imports warehouse, back stairs to private safe room (transition to study)
current_scene_description_before: The study exists as a secluded room within the warehouse complex. A desk with a heavy folder, a revolver resting atop it, and dim, cold lighting. Rain taps at distant windows. Chairs sit in a silent semicircle; Vito, Luca, and Vince are present, the tense weight of unspoken debts lingering in the air. Luca reads aloud, turning pages slowly, the clocking quiet punctuated only by the soft click of the revolver being handled and the distant hum of machinery outside.

speaker: Luca Moretti
line_text: [Luca stands in the hospital corridor, the revolver heavy under his jacket. The door to 214 is still open a crack. Through it, the beeping of machines — steady, slower than this morning. He doesn't go in. Instead he takes out the brass key, turns it over one last time, then slips it into an envelope he pulls from his inside pocket. He writes on the front: 'For Vito. Bakery or not — you'll know where this goes.' He tucks it into the doorframe and walks toward the elevator. The folder stays under his arm. The revolver stays where it is.] I'll be back before sunrise. If the machines go quiet while I'm gone — [he stops, doesn't turn around] — tell him I went to close the books. His way.
new_scene_name: Hospital corridor outside room 214, elevator exit to lobby

He carries the folder, revolver in place, the envelope tucked into the doorframe as a silent farewell note. The corridor’s fluorescent hum persists; rain taps faintly outside. The door to 214 is ajar, machines softly beeping. The elevator door awaits as Luca moves toward a different immediate location within the hospital.

current_scene_description_before: A stark, sterile corridor bathed in harsh fluorescent light. The hum of machines from behind a door, a single plastic chair keeps Luca company as rain taps somewhere distant. He pockets the folder, the revolver heavy under his jacket, and pushes open the door to his father’s room, the weight of debt and forgiveness pressing toward a precipice.

speaker: Luca Moretti
line_text: [Dawn on the docks. Luca stands at the railing, the folder open in one hand, a coffee cooling in the other. The names in the ledger mean nothing to the longshoremen lighting cigarettes a few yards away. But one address — circled in his father's shaking hand — means everything. A warehouse on Pier 7. He closes the folder, drains the coffee, and starts walking. The revolver shifts against his ribs with every step.] Last stop.
new_scene_name: Docks at Pier 7, dawn
new_scene_description: Dawn light over the industrial harbor. Luca stands on a weathered warehouse railing, the folder closed now and tucked under his arm, coffee cooling in his other hand. Longshoremen lean against the rail, cigarettes burning, the foggy water below. A warehouse at Pier 7 looms in the distance; the air carries rust and salt, and the distant clink of chains echoes from inside. The revolver remains pressed against his side as he begins to walk toward the warehouse’s dim mouth.
current_scene_name_before: Hospital corridor outside room 214, elevator exit to lobby
current_scene_description_before: The corridor remains the same, but Luca steps away from the door, heading toward the elevator. He carries the folder, revolver in place, the envelope tucked into the doorframe as a silent farewell note. The corridor’s fluorescent hum persists; rain taps faintly outside. The door to 214 is ajar, machines softly beeping. The elevator door awaits as Luca moves toward a different immediate location within the hospital.

speaker: Luca Moretti
line_text: [The warehouse on Pier 7 is rusted shut. Luca has to shoulder the door twice before it gives. Inside: stacked crates, the smell of salt and diesel — and a single desk at the far end with a lamp burning. A man sits behind it. Older. No suit. The face from the folder — the last name on the list. He doesn't stand. He doesn't flinch. He says: 'Your father told me you'd come. He said you'd either bring the gun or the folder. You brought both.'] [Luca stops ten feet from the desk, hands at his sides.] Then you know more than I do. So start talking before I decide which one to use.
new_scene_name: Pier 7 warehouse interior, dawn confrontation
new_scene_description: A rusted, shut warehouse door groans open, revealing stacked crates and a dim, diesel-scented air. A single desk sits at the far end, lamp burning, an older man seated behind it. Luca stands ten feet away, revolver at his side, folder clutched. The fog outside presses against the cracked windows as tension tightens in the cramped space.
current_scene_name_before: Docks at Pier 7, dawn
current_scene_description_before: Dawn light over the industrial harbor. Luca stands on a weathered warehouse railing, the folder closed now and tucked under his arm, coffee cooling in his other hand. Longshoremen lean against the rail, cigarettes burning, the foggy water below. A warehouse at Pier 7 looms in the distance; the air carries rust and salt, and the distant clink of chains echoes from inside. The revolver remains pressed against his side as he begins to walk toward the warehouse’s dim mouth.

speaker: Vincenzo 'Vince' Marino
line_text: [Vince rises, walks to the window, hands clasped behind his back.] That envelope Luca mentioned — it changes things. My father's health, the loyalties in this room... everyone's got a choice to make. I've made mine. You understand?
new_scene_name: Pier 7 warehouse interior, dawn, Vince steps to window
new_scene_description: Vince rises from his position and walks to the window, hands clasped behind his back. The dim light pools over the foggy glass as he speaks softly, the weight of his words hanging in the crisp dawn air. The room holds its breath as he declares a turning point, the envelope now a pivot point for loyalties and futures.
current_scene_name_before: Pier 7 warehouse interior, dawn confrontation
current_scene_description_before: A rusted, shut warehouse door groans open, revealing stacked crates and a dim, diesel-scented air. A single desk sits at the far end, lamp burning, an older man seated behind it. Luca stands ten feet away, revolver at his side, folder clutched. The fog outside presses against the cracked windows as tension tightens in the cramped space.

speaker: Luca Moretti
line_text: [The man behind the desk smiles — a thin, tired smile. He pushes a photograph across the desk. Luca picks it up. It's his father, maybe twenty years younger, standing next to this man. Both laughing. Both unarmed.] My name is Marco Ferri. I was your father's underboss before you were born. Before the Moretti name meant anything. [He taps the folder.] That's not a hit list. It's a guest list. Every man in there is still loyal to your father — and to whoever he chose to follow him. I'm the last one. I've been waiting ten years for you to walk through that door. [Luca sets the photo down slowly. His hand doesn't go near the revolver.] Ten years. And you picked tonight. [Ferri nods.] Your father's dying. Tonight, someone becomes the Don. I needed to see which one showed up at my door.
new_scene_name: Pier 7 warehouse interior, dawn — confrontation escalates inside
new_scene_description: The scene stays in the warehouse interior. Objects on the desk glow faintly in dawn light; tension tightens as Luca confronts Marco Ferri across the desk. The air tightens with the revelation that the guest list is a trap and that a new Don will be chosen tonight. No physical relocation occurs in this beat; the confrontation unfolds in the same room.
current_scene_name_before: Pier 7 warehouse interior, dawn, Vince steps to window
current_scene_description_before: Vince rises from his position and walks to the window, hands clasped behind his back. The dim light pools over the foggy glass as he speaks softly, the weight of his words hanging in the crisp dawn air. The room holds its breath as he declares a turning point, the envelope now a pivot point for loyalties and futures.

speaker: Vincenzo 'Vince' Marino
line_text: [Vince stops at the door, hand on the frame, not turning around.] I'm going to see my father. That envelope stays sealed until I decide otherwise. Anyone who wants to challenge that... step forward now. You understand?
current_scene_name_before: Pier 7 warehouse interior, dawn — confrontation escalates inside
current_scene_description_before: The scene stays in the warehouse interior. Objects on the desk glow faintly in dawn light; tension tightens as Luca confronts Marco Ferri across the desk. The air tightens with the revelation that the guest list is a trap and that a new Don will be chosen tonight. No physical relocation occurs in this beat; the confrontation unfolds in the same room.

speaker: Luca Moretti
line_text: [Luca pulls out the chair across from Ferri and sits. He doesn't take out the revolver. Instead he opens the folder, spins it around so Ferri can see the last page — the one with the circled address and the final line in his father's hand.] He wrote: 'The only debt left unpaid is the one you owe yourself. Settle it, or don't come back.' [Luca looks up from the page.] I've been trying to figure out all night what debt he meant. Murder? Forgiveness? Leading the family? [He taps the photo of his father and Ferri laughing.] But this — you waiting here, a photograph from twenty years ago, a guest list instead of a hit list — this tells me the debt isn't about them. It's about me. He wanted me to find you. To learn that the family wasn't built on fear. It was built on this. [He gestures at the photo.] On friendship. So tell me, Marco — did I pass the test? Or is this where you tell me I'm not ready and I should walk away?
current_scene_name_before: Pier 7 warehouse interior, dawn — confrontation escalates inside
current_scene_description_before: The scene stays in the warehouse interior. Objects on the desk glow faintly in dawn light; tension tightens as Luca confronts Marco Ferri across the desk. The air tightens with the revelation that the guest list is a trap and that a new Don will be chosen tonight. No physical relocation occurs in this beat; the confrontation unfolds in the same room.

speaker: Luca Moretti
line_text: [Ferri leans back. The chair creaks. He looks at Luca for a long moment — the way you look at a painting you're trying to understand.] Your father told me once that the hardest thing about being Don isn't making the hard calls. It's knowing which calls to make and which to let someone else make. [He reaches into his jacket. Luca's hand moves toward the revolver — but Ferri just pulls out a key. Same brass. Same hand-filed teeth.] You passed when you walked through the door with the folder instead of the gun. This — [he slides the key across the desk] — is what opens. Not a safe. A door. In the basement of the old social club on Mulberry. You go there alone. You go there tonight. What you find will tell you everything. [He stands, picks up his hat.] After that, the choice is yours. Your father's legacy, or your own.
new_scene_name: Mulberry Street social club basement, old social club in the city
new_scene_description: The warehouse interior abruptly fades as a new, colder image settles: Luca stands alone, the key resting on the desk. The scene shifts to a dim basement beneath the old social club on Mulberry Street, where a heavy door awaits—dust motes drift in a single shaft of weak light, and the air tastes of old secrets. Luca pockets the key or contemplates it, the weight of a choice looming as night threatens to fall outside. The room is quiet except for the distant hum of a refrigeration unit and the soft click of a door latch somewhere unseen.
current_scene_name_before: Pier 7 warehouse interior, dawn — confrontation escalates inside
current_scene_description_before: The scene stays in the warehouse interior. Objects on the desk glow faintly in dawn light; tension tightens as Luca confronts Marco Ferri across the desk. The air tightens with the revelation that the guest list is a trap and that a new Don will be chosen tonight. No physical relocation occurs in this beat; the confrontation unfolds in the same room.

speaker: Luca Moretti
line_text: [The old social club on Mulberry is closed, boarded up, condemned. Luca goes around back, finds the basement door under a rusted grate. The brass key turns smooth as silk. The door swings open onto a narrow staircase lit by a single bare bulb. At the bottom: a room. Not a storage room — an office. Preserved. His father's desk from forty years ago. Same leather chair. Same ashtray. Same photograph on the wall — the wedding, the Don and his wife, young, laughing. On the desk: a letter, sealed, in his father's hand. Addressed to Luca. He picks it up. Doesn't open it yet. Just stands there in the quiet, holding the last thing his father ever wrote him.] You could have left this in the safe behind the Madonna. You could have left it with Ferri. But you made me find it. Piece by piece. [He turns the envelope over.] Alright, Popà. Let's see what you couldn't say to my face.
new_scene_name: Mulberry Street basement, back-entrance office reveal
new_scene_description: Luca descends into a narrow staircase illuminated by a single bare bulb. At the bottom sits a preserved office: father’s desk, leather chair, ashtray, wedding photograph, and a sealed letter in his father’s hand. Luca stands in the quiet, holding the envelope, the last thing his father ever wrote him, not yet opened.
current_scene_name_before: Mulberry Street social club basement, old social club in the city
current_scene_description_before: The warehouse interior abruptly fades as a new, colder image settles: Luca stands alone, the key resting on the desk. The scene shifts to a dim basement beneath the old social club on Mulberry Street, where a heavy door awaits—dust motes drift in a single shaft of weak light, and the air tastes of old secrets. Luca pockets the key or contemplates it, the weight of a choice looming as night threatens to fall outside. The room is quiet except for the distant hum of a refrigeration unit and the soft click of a door latch somewhere unseen.

speaker: Vincenzo 'Vince' Marino
line_text: [Vince checks his watch, jaw tight.] I've given everyone time to think. The Don doesn't have that luxury. I'm going to his bedside, and when I come back, I expect answers. Not promises — answers. You understand?
new_scene_name: Mulberry Street basement, back-entrance office reveal (continues) / Vince exits to Don's bedside
new_scene_description: The basement fades as Vince’s line implies a swift relocation: he intends to move from this quiet office to a hospital bedside, cutting away to a new, urgent location. The space ahead is tense and immediate, with the sense of a planned departure and imminent confrontation.
current_scene_name_before: Mulberry Street basement, back-entrance office reveal
current_scene_description_before: Luca descends into a narrow staircase illuminated by a single bare bulb. At the bottom sits a preserved office: father’s desk, leather chair, ashtray, wedding photograph, and a sealed letter in his father’s hand. Luca stands in the quiet, holding the envelope, the last thing his father ever wrote him, not yet opened.

speaker: Luca Moretti
line_text: [Luca sits in his father's old chair. The envelope is light. One page. He unfolds it. His father's handwriting — the same cramped script from the ledger, but shakier, older.] 'Luca — if you're reading this, then Marco kept his word and you kept your head. I never wanted you to carry this weight. I wanted you to be a doctor, a teacher, anything but what I became. But blood doesn't work that way, and neither does love. The family I built was never meant for you. It was meant to buy you a choice I never had. So here it is: the folder is real. The men in it are loyal. The key opens a box in the wall behind this letter — inside is enough to walk away. A new name. A new city. A life. Or you can keep the folder and the revolver and become what I was. I love you. That's why I'm giving you the choice I never had. —Popà' [Luca folds the letter. He sits in the silence for a long time. Then he stands, finds the box in the wall, and opens it.]
new_scene_name: Mulberry Street basement, back-entrance office reveal, then Luca relocates to wall box opening
new_scene_description: Luca sits in his father’s old chair, unfolds a letter, and reads a pivotal confession. He then rises, finds a hidden wall box behind the letter and opens it, revealing a folder, a revolver, and a new life choice. The action shifts from the immediate office space to the discovery of a concealed box within the same basement, but establishes a decisive, physical relocation as he moves to access the box.
current_scene_name_before: Mulberry Street basement, back-entrance office reveal (continues) / Vince exits to Don's bedside
current_scene_description_before: The basement fades as Vince’s line implies a swift relocation: he intends to move from this quiet office to a hospital bedside, cutting away to a new, urgent location. The space ahead is tense and immediate, with the sense of a planned departure and imminent confrontation.

speaker: Luca Moretti
line_text: [The box is small. Inside: a passport, a driver's license, a key to a safety deposit box in a bank three states away. A new name. A new birthday. Clean. Empty. Everything Luca never asked for and a part of him desperately wants. He holds the passport for a long time. Reads the name: 'Michael Rossi.' Good. Simple. American. No weight to carry. Then he sets it down. He picks up his father's revolver — checks the cylinder, spins it, sets it next to the passport. He looks at both for a long time. Then he puts the revolver in his waistband, picks up the folder, and leaves the passport in the box. He closes the lid, locks it, and drops the key in his pocket. On the way out, he pauses at the desk. Takes a pen. Writes on the back of the envelope his father's letter came in: 'Popà — I choose both. The new name, for when this is over. But first, I finish what you started. —Luca' He props it against the photograph of his parents' wedding, turns off the light, and walks up the stairs into the dawn.]
new_scene_name: Mulberry Street basement, wall box opened to reveal a concealed departure plan
new_scene_description: The camera/scene remains in the same basement space but shifts focus to the concealed wall box and Luca’s decision to take a new identity, while placing revolver and letter into play as he prepares to leave. He retrieves the passport, examines it, pockets the revolver, leaves the envelope note propped by a wedding photo, and exits toward the stairs as dawn breaks outside.
current_scene_name_before: Mulberry Street basement, back-entrance office reveal, then Luca relocates to wall box opening
